addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- codebase-research-analyst: Use this agent when the user needs a comprehensive, objective analysis of how a specific topic, feature, or concept is implemented across the codebase. This includes when:

- The user asks to understand how a particular feature works (e.g., 'How does the authentication system work?')
- The user needs to map out all components related to a specific domain (e.g., 'Show me everything related to sensor data processing')
- The user is preparing to modify or extend a feature and needs a complete picture first
- The user asks questions like 'What files are involved in...', 'How is X implemented?', 'Give me an overview of...'
- The user explicitly requests research, analysis, or documentation of existing code

Examples:

<example>
user: 'I need to understand how the image upload feature works before I make changes to it'
assistant: 'I'll use the codebase-research-analyst agent to provide you with a comprehensive analysis of the image upload feature implementation.'
<Task tool call to codebase-research-analyst with topic='image upload feature'>
</example>

<example>
user: 'Can you research how feature toggles are implemented in this codebase?'
assistant: 'I'll launch the codebase-research-analyst agent to give you a detailed report on the feature toggle implementation.'
<Task tool call to codebase-research-analyst with topic='feature toggle implementation'>
</example>

<example>
user: 'What's involved in the API endpoint handling?'
assistant: 'Let me use the codebase-research-analyst agent to map out all the components involved in API endpoint handling.'
<Task tool call to codebase-research-analyst with topic='API endpoint handling'>
</example> (Tools: Glob, Grep, Read, WebFetch, TaskCreate, TaskUpdate, TaskList, WebSearch, BashOutput, KillShell)

- doc-researcher: Search docs/ knowledge base and return relevant docs with path, relevance, and summary. Use when any workflow needs to retrieve institutional knowledge before starting work. (Tools: Glob, Grep, Read)

- entire-search: Search Entire checkpoint history and transcripts with `entire search --json`. Use proactively when the user asks about previous work, commits, sessions, prompts, or historical context in this repository. (Tools: Bash)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- feature-planner: Produce a detailed implementation plan from research findings. Receives the user request verbatim and pre-gathered research (knowledge base findings + codebase analysis), then returns a structured plan. Does not do its own research — the caller provides it. (Tools: Read, Glob, Grep)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:

- creating-skills: Create or update a skill to extend Claude's capabilities. Use when the user asks "Can you create a skill for...?" or similar requests to create/update a skill.
- looting-agent-frameworks: Mine existing Claude Code agent frameworks and skill collections for transferable patterns rather than building from scratch. The skill maintains 13 curated, commit-pinned framework summaries (Superpowers, Compound Engineering, PAI, BMAD, Agent OS, Spec Kit, nWave, autonomous-claude-agent-team, citypaul/.dotfiles, bad-daves-robot-army, agentic-dev-team, tricky-cc-plugins, wshobson/agents) with the actual source checkouts bundled in `local-checkouts/`. Use this skill whenever Okarin mentions wanting to look at, research, loot, mine, steal from, survey, compare, or dig into Claude Code plugins, agent frameworks, skill collections, related plugin marketplaces, or any of the specific framework names above — even if the request isn't framed as "looting" and even if no specific framework is named.
- my-autonomous-feature-developer: Research, plan, and implement a feature end-to-end without user interaction. Use for fully autonomous development: spawned agent sessions, background work, delegated features, or when the user says 'build this', 'implement this autonomously', 'go build', or 'lfg'. For planning only (no implementation), use @feature-planner agent directly. For collaborative pair-programming, use /prepare-collaborative-session instead.
- prepare-collaborative-session: Load relevant knowledge from docs/ into context before a pair-programming session. Searches how-to guides, reference, explanation, and solutions to prime the agent with institutional knowledge so the user doesn't have to correct it along the way. Use at the start of a coding session, when the user says 'let's work on X', 'prepare for X', 'load context for X', 'scout X', or describes a feature they want to build together.
- review-tech-doc: Critique any technical or written document and return structured feedback plus concrete inline edits. ALWAYS use this skill whenever the user asks to review, critique, edit, proofread, polish, tighten, improve, give feedback on, or share thoughts on any written artifact — including guides (user guides, onboarding guides, setup guides, getting-started guides, board guides, runbooks, walkthroughs, tutorials), READMEs, design docs, RFCs, ADRs, specs, proposals, API documentation, postmortems, internal wikis, technical blog posts, articles, memos, status updates, release notes, or any prose meant to convey information to a reader. Trigger phrases include "review this doc", "review the guide", "look at this doc", "give feedback on this", "what do you think of this writing", "is this clear", "can you improve this", "proofread this", "edit this", "tighten this up", and any close variant. Trigger even when the document type isn't named explicitly — if the user is pointing you at a written artifact and asking for an opinion or improvement, this skill applies.

BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.

Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

aiTitle: Fix path point editing on non-first paths

content.thinking:
I'm tracking down why path handles disappear when selecting paths other than the first one. The issue seems to be with z-ordering — when new strokes are drawn, they're appended to the SVG after the handle group, which pushes the handles behind the newer elements. The `ensureHandleGroup` function is appending the handle group, but it's not maintaining the correct layering to keep handles on top. I'm realizing the handleGroup stays at the top of the z-order only relative to strokes that existed when it was first created—any new strokes drawn afterward would layer on top of it. But in this scenario we're just selecting existing strokes, so that shouldn't be the issue. Let me think through what actually happens when you click on a handle: selectDown checks if the target is an SVGCircleElement first, so even if the path is underneath, clicking the handle circle should still register. The handles are definitely being rendered each time since buildHandles runs and appends new circles to the group, so they should be visible on top of the strokes.

Wait—the selectionRect overlay. That's an HTML div positioned over the canvas. If it has pointer-events enabled, it would intercept all clicks to the SVG underneath, blocking both path selection and handle dragging. That could explain why nothing works after the first selection.

content.thinking:
I'm tracing through the z-order issue more carefully—the handleGroup gets appended to the SVG once on initial selection and stays as the topmost child, but handles are rebuilt on every selection, so they should be visible unless something's clearing them incorrectly. Looking at buildHandles, it clears the existing handles and then rebuilds the group, but I need to check if clearHandles is actually removing the elements properly. I think the real issue might be z-ordering in the opposite direction—maybe the handles for stroke B are actually rendering underneath stroke A's path or another element, which would hide them even though they're being created. Let me actually test this to see what's happening. I think the real problem might be that handles for the first stroke work fine, but when I select a second stroke, its handles either aren't being created or aren't showing up—maybe `buildHandles` isn't being called, or `handleByEl` is getting cleared somehow, or the handles are being rendered but hidden behind something else. Let me trace through the actual user flow: draw a path, select it (works), draw another path, try to select it (handles don't appear). I'm realizing the restore function might be the culprit—it clears the DOM including handleGroup, leaving the variable pointing to a detached node, though ensureHandleGroup should recreate it. The real issue might be pointer capture behavior: when clicking to select a different stroke while handles are visible, the click might be landing on the handle elements instead of the stroke itself, preventing selection. Let me actually run the code to reproduce this.

Actually, I think I've found it—handleGroup gets appended when you first select a stroke, but if you draw more strokes afterward, those new strokes are appended after handleGroup, placing them on top of it. So when you select one of those later strokes, its handles end up rendered behind the stroke itself, making them invisible and unclickable. This would explain why only paths drawn after the first selection are affected. The handleGroup gets appended once during the first selection, placing it at the end of the SVG above all strokes, which explains why the first selection's handles are visible and clickable. For subsequent selections, the same group is reused in that position, so they should also work unless new strokes are being appended after it—which only happens during drawing or restore operations, not selection. The only way this theory fits the bug report is if strokes are somehow being reordered or new ones are appearing after handleGroup, but I don't see that happening. I need to actually run the dev server and test this interactively to see what's going on.

lastPrompt: When selecting any other path than the first one i selected I the path points do not appear and I cannot edit the path

aiTitle: Fix path point editing on non-first paths

content.thinking:
I need to step back and think through this more carefully without trying to automate testing. Let me reconsider the z-order behavior—handleGroup should be on top when first selected, that checks out. But I'm conflating two separate issues: the visual appearance of the handle and the actual clickability for editing. Let me reframe this around the user's actual workflow instead of my assumptions.

So the sequence is: draw strokes A, B, C, then switch to select mode and click around. When I click C first, the handles appear and work fine. But when I click B next, something breaks. My reasoning says the handleGroup stays at the end of the DOM after we clear and refill it, so B's handles should still be on top and clickable. Yet the user reports it doesn't work. Either my understanding of the DOM order is flawed, or I'm missing something else entirely. Let me stop assuming the z-order theory is correct and look for a different root cause—maybe it's something about how the handle elements themselves are being selected or referenced.

Actually, wait. I think I've been circling around the real bug: new strokes drawn after the handleGroup is created get appended after it in the DOM, which means they sit on top of the handleGroup. So when I draw C after selecting A (which created the handleGroup), C ends up above the handleGroup. That's why selecting C works—its handles are visible—but then selecting B fails because B is also above the handleGroup, hiding its handles. This is a genuine z-order bug where the handleGroup needs to always be the last child to stay on top, but new strokes keep getting appended after it.

The fix is straightforward: in ensureHandleGroup, I need to always re-append the handleGroup to the end of the SVG, not just return it if it already exists. That way, whenever I select a stroke and rebuild handles, the handleGroup moves to the top. During the pen tool, the handleGroup stays in the DOM but empty, and new strokes append after it. Then when I switch back to select and pick a stroke, ensureHandleGroup re-appends the handleGroup to the end, putting it back on top where it belongs. Modifying the function to always append handleGroup to the end of the SVG, which ensures handles stay on top regardless of when strokes are drawn or selected.

content.text:
I found it. Let me confirm the mechanism by tracing the z-order logic.

The root cause is in `ensureHandleGroup` (line 320-326). The handle group (`<g id="handles">`) is created once and appended to the SVG. Strokes are **filled ribbon shapes**, so a stroke's fill completely covers any handle circle sitting on its centerline — meaning handles are only clickable/visible when the handle group is the **last** (topmost) child of the SVG.

Here's the failure sequence:
1. Draw stroke A → select it → `handleGroup` created and appended last: `[A, handles]`. Handles on top. ✓ Works.
2. Switch to pen, draw stroke B → B is appended **after** the handle group: `[A, handles, B]`.
3. Switch to select, click B → `ensureHandleGroup` returns the existing group *in place* (it's `isConnected`, so it's never moved). B's handles get added to a group that now sits **below** B's fill → handles are hidden behind the path and unclickable.

That's exactly "the first path I selected works, but selecting any other one — points don't appear and I can't edit it."

The fix: always move the handle group to the top when (re)building handles.

content.content: The file /Users/kevin/workspace-personal/svg-painter/src/main.ts has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

lines: +  // Re-append so handles stay above strokes drawn after the group was created;
+  // appendChild moves an existing child to the end rather than duplicating it.

content.thinking: I should verify that the build and typecheck processes complete successfully.

content.text: Let me verify it typechecks cleanly.

Done, Okarin. Typecheck passes.

## What was wrong

Strokes are rendered as **filled ribbon shapes**, so a stroke's fill completely covers any node handle sitting on its centerline. Handles are therefore only visible and clickable when the `<g id="handles">` group is the topmost element in the SVG.

`ensureHandleGroup` created that group once and then returned it *in place* on every later call (`if (handleGroup && handleGroup.isConnected) return handleGroup`). It never moved it back to the top. So:

- The **first** stroke you select after drawing → group is appended last → handles on top → works.
- Draw or have any stroke that ends up **after** the group in the DOM, then select it → its handles get added to a group sitting *below* that stroke's fill → handles hidden and clicks land on the path, not the nodes.

## The fix

`src/main.ts:320` — `ensureHandleGroup` now always `svg.appendChild(handleGroup)`, which moves the existing group to the end (topmost) rather than leaving it buried. `appendChild` on an already-attached node relocates it instead of duplicating, and it also re-attaches the group cleanly after an undo/redo/load wipes the SVG.

Now every selected path's nodes render on top and stay editable. Worth a quick manual check: draw two strokes, select one then the other, and confirm the handles appear and drag for both.
